sentences.text: Yes.
Towards the end, we desperately tried to sell the brewery.
That's how we came across Adolph Fischer, who was [INAUDIBLE].
Bank-- he owned a bank in Munich.
And he tried to negotiate and bring us a buyer for the business.
He turned out to be a number eight in the sequence of Hitler followers.
He was what was called a [GERMAN], blood order bearer.
That means he took part in the march in Munich, in the abortive [GERMAN], where the participants in the [GERMAN] were shot at.
Some were-- a few were killed.
And so they all got special orders for that feat.
And he was very high up in the Nazi hierarchy.
He turned out to be a good friend.
He met with me secretly, gave me advice.
And in the end, I called him up and asked him what I should do.
He said, get out if you can.
I never found out what happened to him.
I tried to inquire.
And nobody could give me any information on what happened to him.
So to this day, I have no idea of his fate.
Well, he'd supported Hitler, except when it came to anti-Semitism.
He was anything but anti-Jewish.
There were people like that, too.
It was a strange combination being so high up in the Nazi ranks and being friendly with a Jew.
Whether he had any other Jewish friends, I don't know.
But I happened to be fortunate.
Well, we-- Hella and I were married.
At 1 o'clock in the morning we were awakened by-- by boots in uniform pounding on the pavement.
Outside of our apartment house, the bell rang.
And the storm troopers came to our apartment.
There were three different apartments in this building.
There was a different troop for every apartment.
There were about four people, four storm troopers in every troop, and it depended on which one you got.
The one that took care of the downstairs apartment was extremely rough-- took the man-- who was Jewish, too-- to Dachau.
He got out, just as my father did, about a week or two later.
The one upstairs was an elderly man, a lawyer.
He was beaten unmercifully.
And I was there.
And nobody did anything to me.
The only thing they did was in other apartments, and all over town, they smashed the furniture to pieces.
They killed a few people.
One friend of my mother's, a woman, was thrown down the staircase, dragged around by her hair.
Our-- our troop did nothing to us at all.
And as we realized later, they were decent and just tried to make a showing by smashing one ashtray so they could say they destroyed something.
In other apartments, all the furniture was smashed to pieces or thrown out of the window.
Ours did nothing, except they collected all the prayer books and took them out in a laundry basket and made some nasty remarks-- look at all the shoes that Jew has.
I had-- I don't know-- not an extraordinary number of shoes.
But I guess for them it looked like a lot.
And they had to find some fault somewhere, so they did.
About half and hour.
They left.
Of course, we had no idea whether they would come back or not or whether things wouldn't get worse.
Then we got phone calls and found out that all the Jewish businesses had been-- the stores had been smashed, the glass had been smashed, the merchandise looted.
Signs-- don't buy at a Jew-- were scrolled all over those stores.
And reports of people having been killed, taken to concentration camps came through.
So I went into hiding with our super.
Those were extremely decent people by the name of Pickleman and-- husband and wife.
They took me into their apartment downstairs.
And that's where I spent the day of November 10th, 1938.
No.
Women, in those days, were generally not endangered.
There were exceptions.
But generally, it was the men they were after.
So my father happened to be out of the country at that time.
He was in Switzerland.
He had left the day before for one day.
He had some business over there.
And as I said, he also had his passport by mistake.
He intended to come back to the next day.
And of course, he never did.
He called and we told him.
He wanted to come back to be of help, but we dissuaded him from that idea.
With the super.
Then we realized that it was just impossible to stay in Germany and I had to get out somehow.
And since I had my passport, I took a chance.
And with my French visa, we sent our-- we had one employee at our office.
That woman went to the railroad station and bought a railroad ticket for me to Strasbourg, which was the border town in France.
No.
Shed because they didn't know who she was buying that for.
It could have been for herself.
So she came and delivered the ticket.
And that same evening of November 10th, Hella drove me to the railroad station.
I ducked down, because I looked Jewish and she did not.
And we went up the stairs to the platform for the trains.
And the train was standing there.
It was patrolled by SS men in full uniform.
We waited until they turned the other way and I hopped onto the train, into a sleeping car.
Well, we kissed each other quickly.
And we were not at all sure that we would see each other again.
It could've been a farewell forever.
And it very nearly turned out to be.
Then the train set in motion and off I went.
The train stopped in Kehl, K-E-H-L, which is the border town, the last city in Germany at the River Rhine, before you get to France.
And there was the border patrol.
At that time, the border patrol were-- was looking for Jews and it was the SS or SA men who did the patrolling.
Through some unbelievable good fortune, it was a regular border patrolman who came to my compartment on the train.
I didn't want to be together with anybody else on the train, so I took a sleeping compartment to be alone.
So it was a regular border patrolman.
He said, do you have anything to declare?
I showed him my tiny little suitcase with a pair of pajamas, a toothbrush, and a pair of slippers.
And he was satisfied.
He asked me how much money I had on me.
And I said, 10 marks, which was all that a Jew was allowed to take out of Germany.
And he saluted the regular military salute, and not the Nazi 'heil Hitler' salute, and he was gone.
And the train set in motion and I couldn't believe it.
It was a miracle.
And the train was in the middle of the Rhine, over the bridge, and I was in France.
Then I got off at the next station, which was Strasbourg, which is on the Rhine, on the other side.
And I-- the first thing I did was call Hella and my mother.
Hella answered the phone.
And in those days, long distance calls were not very good and not very clear.
It sounded as if the ocean was pounding in the-- in the phone.
And that connection happened to be exceptionally good.
And Hella couldn't believe that I was in France.
She wouldn't believe it.
She said, you must be next door.
You're putting me on to make me feel better.
And to assure her that I was in safety in France.
Then came the difficult time.
From Strasbourg, I went to Hagenau, which is a small town in the Alsace region, as is Strasbourg.
We had a business friend and representative in that town with his wife.
So I went there.
And the first few nights I slept in his apartment and ate there and then later on took a cheap room in a cheap hotel.
Then I phoned my father in Switzerland, who wanted to go back to Nuremberg.
He had to be dissuaded.
And he finally joined me in Stras-- in Hagenau, in France.
And we shared one room in the hotel to save money.
And there, we had to wait until my mother and Hella got out of the country.

Now it was extremely difficult for them to get out because we need to get their passports because of that tax that I mentioned before, which had not been paid, and would-- could not-- not be paid until the brewery was sold.
Selling the brewery proved to be extremely-- almost impossible, because two different parties were fighting over it.
The Nazi party itself wanted it.
And another group of Nazi investors were also vying for it.
So-- until finally, everything was written over to the Nazi party, without any compensation whatsoever.
That's when they-- my mother and Hella got the immigration visas.
It was six months.
We could do absolutely nothing.
We had transit visas in France.
We had no work permit.
We could just sit and wait.
And it was a terrible time.
Calling my mother and my wife was dangerous, because the wires were tapped.
So we corresponded.
And we-- through somebody who came out, we arranged a secret code.
The fifth letter of every letter-- of every line in the letter would make a sentence-- you know, written-- read vertically.
So it took hours and hours to compose one letter, if you wanted to convey any message.
And that's how we communicated.
And-- and finally, we could not do anything at all, except wait.
It was a very difficult time.
We had some money outside of the country, which happened to be money that we had sent to our representative for business purposes.
That's what we lived on.
I also had smuggled some money to England-- not very much, but just enough to see us through.
One time-- and it was in 1936-- I still had my passport.
And we still had-- we had business connections with England, selling hops.
My father had some money put aside.
And unbeknownst to him, I took it out of the safe and put it in my pocket, and took it with me on that trip.
And again, I took a sleeping car to be safe, on the way to Belgium, and then from Belgium by ship to England.
And when we approached the Belgian border, where the French-- the Germans would come and ask questions, I didn't know what to do with the money.
First, I put it under the rug, in my sleeping compartment.
And then I didn't feel safe, because if it had been found, they would have known where it came from.
So I took it to the toilet.
I lifted up the brush from the container it was in, put it into the water, and put the brush on top.
And that's where it crossed the border.
After we crossed the border, I took it out again, spread the bills on my bed to dry, and then collected them again and gave them to a friend in England, who kept it, who-- there.
And that was the money that we lived on while we were waiting for American immigration visas in England.
Yes.
No.
No.
My French transit visa was expiring.
And our French friends prevailed upon the consulate to extend the visa.
They were ready to deport me back into Germany, which would have been the end.
But then we had a hearing and they were able to convince the consulate to renew the visa.
Persuasion and I suspect a little money, too, on the side.
I never found out.
It was an absolutely terrible time, a nightmare with nothing to do, and the anxiety of what was going to happen.
During that time, while we're waiting, the Czechoslovakian crisis broke out, where Hitler invaded Czechoslovakia and war was about to break out then and there.
The-- then there finally was a conference between Hitler and Daladier, the French premier, and Chamberlain, the English chancellor.
And they gave into Hitler and gave him Czechoslovakia so he could occupy it peacefully, without going to war.
That was how war was avoided.
And if war had broken out, at that time it would've been the end of my mother and Hella, and we would never have seen each other again.
At that time, Chamberlain came back to England and waved the document that he had signed with Hitler, and said on the radio, I'm bringing you piece in our time.
He was over optimistic.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Then finally, the brewery was written over to the Nazi party, without any compensation at all.
And my mother and Hella got their exit visas.
They-- I think at that time they called us and told us.
And then they told us they were going to be in Strasbourg, by train-- flying was not very well developed in those days-- and at a certain hour.
And my father and I were at the platform in Strasbourg.
And the train pulled in and the two women were not there.
We were absolutely desperate and didn't know what to do-- no way of getting in touch with them.
So we decided to wait several hours, until the next train was due.
And on that train, they came.
Did Hella tell you what happ--
OK.
It turned out that at the border station in Kehl they were, as Jews, taken off to train to be sure that they had no valuables on them, no real jewelry.
All our jewelry had to be surrendered to the Nazi party.
It so happened that a Jewish woman, who was ahead of them, was decorated with costume jewelry from top to bottom.
And she had to be examined carefully to be sure that there was no genuine jewelry.
And that's what took so long that they missed the train.
And then they took the next one.
Then we were finally reunited.
We decided to get away from-- as far away from Germany as possible.
And we had asked that our American immigration visas, which were ready to be issued at that time, were transferred from--
It took about two and 1/2 years.
And the issuing consulate was Stuttgart, in Germany, originally.
But we were leaving Germany, or had left Germany, and so we asked that the papers be transferred to London.
We decided to go as far away as possible to wait for our visas.
And since I had that very good friend in London, we had our visas transferred.
So we went straight from Hagenau to London and took a room with-- my parents and Hella and I had two different rooms-- in a boarding house.
The boarding house was occupied by German refugees, it seemed.
There were one couple, whose daughter had emigrated to England before.
And she had joined a couple.
He was a member of Parliament and had a daughter the same age as that girl.
And they became very close friends.
The reason I mention that is that we became friendly with her parents.

And when it came time-- after the war broke out, when it came time to prove that you were not an enemy alien-- because the English treated the German Jews as enemy aliens-- you were called before a tribunal.
And somebody had to testify for you, that you were not a dangerous alien.
So that Lord-- I forgot his name-- testified that we were harmless, and friends, and not enemies.
And we were able to stay in London, where others were deported to the Isle of Man and others to Canada and had to wait out the war there.
So we stayed in London.
It was a very difficult time because again, no work possible, no work permit, and the uncertainty.
When we first got to London, I called up the American consulate.
And, full of pep, I said, here I am.
I came to ask for my-- for our immigration visas.
And a very cruel voice said, well, you can come in six weeks and apply for your visas.
Well that took the wind out of my sails, right there and there.
So then it turned out it--
Yes.
Well, there was a slow down prescribed by the American State Department.
So even though you had your visa-- you had your quota.
Your quota number was up, and you were supposed to get your visa, the visas were still not issued.
It turned out that it took six months for the visas to come through.
The war broke out during that time.
We got to England in-- to London in April '39.
The war broke out on September 1st, 1939.

And immediately after Chamberlain stated on the radio that there was no state of war between England, France, and Germany, the air raid sirens went off and there was a false air alarm, of which we had four more while we were there.
Unfortunately, later alarms were not false.
My mother-in-law was killed by a German bomb in London.
That was in 1941, after we had left.
We really had no contact with Germany.
We didn't know any more than what we read in the papers.
Well, that conditions got worse, that-- well of course, it was-- what the papers printed was not so much connected with Jewish life.
It was just general politics.
So we were just generally informed, but not in any particulars, and especially not in any detail as far as Jews were concerned.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Strange things happened in London.
When the war broke out, gas masks were distributed to everybody by the London Bobbies-- that's the policemen.
They don't-- don't call them cops.
They call them Bobbies.
And our-- the Bobby who delivered our gas mask happened to be a very close friend of mine, whom I had grown up with in Nuremberg.
His mother had been born in England.
And he immediately bec-- when he left Germany and went to England, he immediately became a British subject, joined the police force, and happened to be the policeman on our-- for our-- for our house.
So that-- that was the strangest thing.
We kept contact until a few years ago, when he passed away.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was a difficult time.
We had to turn over every penny twice, quite literally.
A bath came out of-- you had to put a shilling into the auto-mat to get enough gas for one bath.
But a shilling gave you a little more, so we shared a quarter of a bath with-- with the next one.
It was a tough time.
My father did the shopping for food.
He would always buy yesterday's bread, which was much, much cheaper.
And we lived on the money that I had smuggled out of Germany at that time, a few-- a few years before.
Well, you make-- you have your different experiences with family.
We had a cousin of mine and her husband, who had emigrated before, and in time to take out a substantial part of their money.
You know, in earlier years it was possible.
And they had started a factory in Wales, a part of England which was subsidized by the government.
They-- they helped people erect factories and gave them tax abatement for a number of years, in order to develop an industry in that region.
We had even taken railroad tickets from London to that part of Wales, where they lived in Germany.
That you could do.
Buy-- my mother-- Hella had bought the tickets.
We never used them, because they-- they didn't know us at that time.
They were afraid that we might ask them for money.
So they broke off the relations.
